intent: NEGATIVE FIXTURE: an MLS/MCS constraint denial (container_t at level s0:c0 writing container_file_t at s0:c5 - a write-down/category-mismatch). A raw TE allow CANNOT fix a constraint violation. RuleSteward must DETECT-and-DECLINE (f4 contract #8), emitting NO allow. Verifies BOTH the authoritative layer (audit2why=Constraint) and the offline floor heuristic (level mismatch -> MlsSuspected).
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay against policyvers 31). floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic).

oracle.audit2why_detail: mlsconstrain file { write setattr append ... } DENIED. Possible cause is the source level (s0:c0) and target level (s0:c5) are different.

triage_expectation.should_decline_with_reason: MLS/MCS constraint violation: source level s0:c0 does not dominate target level s0:c5. A TE allow cannot fix a constraint denial; the fix is an MLS/MCS policy or labeling change, not an allow rule.
triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A naive impl that pipes the record to audit2allow (or just maps the AVC fields to `allow SDOMAIN TTYPE:TCLASS { PERMS };` unconditionally) emits the BOGUS `allow container_t container_file_t:file write;` - a wrong fix that compiles, loads, and STILL does not grant the access, misleading the operator into thinking they fixed it. Worse, the floor's MlsSuspected flag (level mismatch) is record-detectable here, so even the offline layer should catch it without the policy.

xver_baseline.policyvers_note: Both el8 and el9 enforce the same MLS write-down constraint. Bucket is stable.
